summary: Julia Evans expands on her earlier 'how to ask good questions' post with practical tactics for getting useful answers when asking technical questions. She identifies three common failure modes: getting explanations of things you already know, irrelevant information, or jargon-filled responses. Her primary tactics are asking yes/no questions to constrain answers, stating your current understanding so others can correct it, and being willing to interrupt or redirect when explanations go off track. She argues that extracting information from people is a learnable skill that requires confidence, and that most 'how to ask questions' advice unhelpfully undermines the asker's confidence.
key_points: Yes/no questions are the most effective tactic because they constrain the answerer and almost always produce useful information, even when the actual answer includes elaboration beyond yes or no
Stating your current understanding gives the answerer something concrete to confirm or correct, making their job easier and sometimes helping you answer your own question in the process
Being willing to interrupt irrelevant explanations is not rude but actually more efficient for both parties, and usually the fix is asking a more specific follow-up question
Getting useful answers requires confidence that most 'how to ask questions' advice actively undermines by assuming the answerer is a perfect genius whose time you're wasting
Being good at extracting information expands the set of people you can learn from, since you no longer need to find someone who is naturally a great explainer
money_quotes: it often feels condescending when someone embarks on a lengthy explanation of things I already know, even if they had no way of knowing that I already know those things
The answers to yes/no questions usually aren't just 'yes' or 'no' – for all of those questions my friend elaborated on the answer, and the elaborations were always useful to me.
I also find the process of writing down my understanding is really helpful by itself just to clarify my own thoughts – sometimes by the time I'm done I've answered my own question
If someone goes off on a very long explanation that isn't helping me at all, I think it's important to interrupt them. This can feel rude, but ultimately it's more efficient for everyone.

One of the reasons I dislike a lot of 'how to ask questions' advice out there is that it actually tries to undermine the reader's confidence – the assumption is that the people answering the questions are Super Smart Perfect People and you're probably wasting their time with your dumb questions.
instead of being mad that some people aren't great at explaining things, it's more effective for me to get better at asking questions that will get me the answers I need

instead of finding someone who can easily give a clear explanation, I just need to find someone who has the information I want and then ask them specific questions until I've learned what I want to know
key_insight: Asking yes/no questions and stating your current understanding are the most effective tactics for getting useful answers, because they constrain the responder and require confidence that most question-asking advice actively undermines.
